title: Persistent inbox polling: /inbox <interval> survives session restart
description: When the user runs '/inbox 5m' (or any interval), persist the polling interval to .claude/beadle.local.md. Add a SessionStart hook that reads this file and calls CronCreate to re-register the /inbox cron automatically. Result: inbox polling resumes on every new session without the user needing to re-run the command. The /inbox status command should show whether polling is active and whether a cron is currently registered.

title: fix(inbox): /inbox auto-poll cron — nonexistent description field, session-only
description:
## Problem

commands/inbox.md #123 claims /inbox 10m creates an autonomous CronCreate loop that runs /inbox every N minutes. Two bugs make it unreliable:

1. **Nonexistent CronCreate.description field.** The spec says to tag the cron job with description /inbox auto-poll and later find it via CronList with that description. The CronCreate tool schema has no description parameter — only cron, prompt, recurring, durable. CronList renders as <id> — <interval> (recurring) [session-only]: <prompt>. The only durable identifier is the prompt. Cleanup logic is broken and will either fail to find the job or create duplicates on repeated /inbox NNm invocations.

2. **Session-only cron.** CronCreate defaults to durable=false. When the Claude session exits, the cron dies. Next session starts with server-side polling configured but no Claude-side loop to process it.

## Fix

Rewrite commands/inbox.md argument-routing section to remove the description field references, match auto-poll jobs by prompt suffix : /inbox, use durable: true so the cron survives session restarts, and tighten delete-before-create ordering so two consecutive /inbox 10m calls leave exactly one cron.

## Verification

1. Fresh session: /inbox 10m → CronList shows a recurring durable job with prompt /inbox.
2. /inbox 10m twice in a row → still exactly one job.
3. /inbox n → job is deleted.
4. Exit session, restart → CronList still shows the job.
close_reason: Merged in PR #128 (squash 0d394a7). /inbox NNm now creates a durable CronCreate job with prompt-based cleanup; autonomous loop verified end-to-end on the post-merge main branch.

title: list_messages: sender email not discoverable for unknown contacts
description:
Beadle has no way to surface a sender's email address for someone who is not yet in the address book. This blocks contact bootstrap on a fresh install.

Facts:
- list_messages shows the display name from the From header (e.g. 'J Freeman') but NOT the email address.
- check_trust returns trust level + permission state but NOT the email address.
- show_mime returns the MIME part tree (type, size) but NOT the headers.
- read_message refuses with 'permission denied: no read permission for sender <email>' — the email is in the ERROR message but not in any success response for an unpermissioned sender.

DES-012 claims redacted listings 'let the owner discover unknown senders and decide whether to add them to contacts.' In practice discovery is blocked: the owner can see that an unknown sender exists but cannot learn enough about them (email address) to add them to the address book.

Verified 2026-04-08 on a fresh Linux install during beadle-1aj troubleshooting. With 285 unread messages in INBOX from 7 different display names, list_messages showed the display names, check_trust and show_mime returned nothing useful, and read_message on each returned the same error. The only way to learn the actual sender address was to parse the permission-denied error message.

Proposed fixes (any one would close the bead):

1. list_messages includes the email address next to the display name in the FROM column. Format: 'J Freeman <notifications@github.com>' or two columns. Today it only shows 'J Freeman'.

2. A dedicated 'unknown_senders' MCP tool that returns a grouped list of (email, display_name_samples, message_count, first_seen, last_seen) for all senders not in contacts. Single tool call, everything needed to decide what permissions to grant.

3. Loosen the permission gate on the From header specifically: list_messages always shows both name AND email for unpermissioned senders, while still redacting subject/body. The From header is not sensitive content — it is the identifier the owner needs to add a contact.

Option 3 is the minimum viable fix and matches the intent of DES-012. Option 2 is the ergonomic fix. Option 1 is the middle ground.

Related: the display name being misleading is filed as a separate bead (notifications@github.com shows up as 'J Freeman', 'Copilot', etc. based on who triggered the event).

feat(config): add smtp_host and TLS config for non-Bridge SMTP
description:
## Problem

beadle-email's SMTP sender is hardcoded to connect to localhost (127.0.0.1) for Proton Bridge. There is no way to configure an external SMTP host. This blocks Docker deployments without Proton Bridge.

## Required Changes

- Add smtp_host field to Config (default: 127.0.0.1 for backwards compat)
- Add smtp_tls field (default: false — Bridge uses STARTTLS on localhost; external SMTP typically uses TLS on 465 or STARTTLS on 587)
- Add smtp_username field (Bridge uses IMAP credentials; external SMTP may differ)
- Update SMTP sender to use configured host/port/TLS
- Update email.json schema docs and README

## Acceptance Criteria

- Config with smtp_host=smtp.fastmail.com smtp_port=587 smtp_tls=true sends successfully
- Default config (no smtp_host) behaves identically to current Bridge behavior
- Tests: unit test config parsing; integration test skipped without live SMTP

## Depends On

Nothing — standalone change

title: SessionStart hook never fires: empty-string matcher rejected by Claude Code
description:
Beadle's .claude-plugin/hooks/hooks.json SessionStart entry declares 'matcher: ""' (empty string). Claude Code rejects the empty-string matcher and never fires the hook.

Consequence: the beadle session-start.sh hook has NEVER successfully run across v0.8.0 → v0.9.0 → v0.10.0. It was supposed to:
1. Deploy /inbox, /mail, /send, /contacts top-level commands to ~/.claude/commands/
2. Write auto-allow permission rules (mcp__plugin_beadle_email__*, mcp__beadle-email__*, Skill(inbox), Skill(mail), Skill(send), Skill(contacts)) to ~/.claude/settings.json
3. Emit additionalContext at session start so Claude knows beadle is ready and has an inbox to check

None of these happened across multiple installs and restarts dating back to the first beadle install on this machine (2026-03-30, original gitCommitSha 0226725). Verified 2026-04-08:
- jq '.permissions.allow | map(select(. | test("beadle"; "i")))' ~/.claude/settings.json returned [] — zero beadle rules
- Running the hook manually with CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT set succeeds, writes all 6 rules, and deploys all 4 commands on the first try. The hook code itself is correct — only the hooks.json matcher declaration is broken.

Also confirmed: every OTHER Punt Labs plugin (biff, quarry, ethos, dungeon, vox, lux) either omits the matcher field entirely on SessionStart OR uses specific event values like 'startup' or 'resume|compact'. Beadle is the only one using 'matcher: ""'. The empty string is silently rejected — no Claude Code error message, just a silent no-op.

ROOT CAUSE: At some point in beadle's history the SessionStart entry was written with 'matcher: ""' instead of either omitting the field or using a valid matcher value. Likely copy-paste from the PostToolUse entry where a matcher IS required.

FIX: Remove the 'matcher: ""' line from the SessionStart entry in .claude-plugin/hooks/hooks.json. One-line change. Then beadle will start firing the hook on every session start and the three downstream effects (commands deployed, permissions allowed, additionalContext emitted) will work automatically.

DOWNSTREAM EFFECTS of the fix:
- New users (fresh beadle install) immediately get /inbox, /mail, /send, /contacts as top-level commands
- The MCP tools and skills are auto-allowed so the user doesn't see permission prompts for beadle tools
- At session start Claude sees 'Beadle plugin setup: ...' additionalContext, knows the inbox exists

SCOPE NOTE: the hook was already silent on the happy path (no actions = no additionalContext). For a post-first-run session with nothing to deploy, the fix alone doesn't cause Claude to check the beadle inbox. That's a SEPARATE improvement — we should consider emitting a persistent 'Beadle inbox ready. Check /inbox.' reminder like biff emits 'Biff session resumed. Check /read.' via its session-resume.sh. Track in a follow-up bead.

Discovered during session troubleshooting on 2026-04-08 after Jim asked why I checked biff inbox but not beadle. Root cause: the beadle session-start instructions never existed because the hook never fired.

title: harden install.sh: detect and warn on uninstall failure across all sibling scripts
description:
Per Copilot review on PR #91 (https://github.com/punt-labs/beadle/pull/91#discussion_r3049386284) and Cursor Bugbot:

The current uninstall-before-install pattern in install.sh:149 fully suppresses errors via '< /dev/null 2>/dev/null || true'. If 'claude plugin uninstall' fails for any reason (subcommand removed, network error, permission issue), the subsequent 'claude plugin install' may still short-circuit and the user remains pinned to the old version with no visible hint that the upgrade silently failed.

This is a cross-cutting concern affecting ALL four sibling install scripts:
- biff/install.sh:138       (biff-8hg1)
- quarry/install.sh:175     (quarry-9ipx)
- vox/install.sh:169        (vox-2fj)
- beadle/install.sh:149     (this bead, beadle-2nk)

Standards update tracked in punt-labs/punt-kit#206 (merged) and #207 (soften+fix).

Hardened approach (must stay consistent across all four scripts):
1. Detect installed state before uninstall (via 'claude plugin list' parse or by reading ~/.claude/plugins/installed_plugins.json)
2. Skip uninstall entirely if not installed (no error to suppress on a fresh machine)
3. Run uninstall for installed case; if it fails, propagate and fail loudly with an actionable message
4. If install fails after a successful uninstall, surface a clear error — recovery story (whether the previous cache directory persists or is deleted) is not yet characterised

First repo to land the hardened pattern becomes the canonical reference; the others backport. Standards doc gets updated once one implementation is validated.

Note on multi-version cache cohabit: Claude Code keeps a versioned cache directory per installed version (~/.claude/plugins/cache/<org>/<plugin>/<version>/) — multiple versions cohabit by design, and installed_plugins.json records which one is active. So a failed install does not literally vaporize the old plugin from disk; the bead is primarily about the FIRST failure mode (silent uninstall failure leaves user pinned).

title: bd sync --flush-only pre-commit hook exports owner PII to tracked JSONL
description:
## Problem

The .git/hooks/pre-commit hook runs bd sync --flush-only on every commit. bd's DB has an 'owner' field per issue (set automatically from git config or bd config when an issue is touched by a given session). The flush writes every 'owner' field to .beads/issues.jsonl — including personal email addresses for older beads that historically never had an owner field in the tracked file.

## Impact

PR #128 landed 49 instances of one contributor's personal email and 30 of another across 79 bead records. Main had 0 owner fields before; now has 79. Verified via:

  git show b55bf27:.beads/issues.jsonl | grep -c '"owner"'   → 0
  git show 0d394a7:.beads/issues.jsonl | grep -c '"owner"'   → 79

This regression is NOT specific to PR #128 — any future bd-touching PR will re-trigger the flush and keep owner PII in sync with the bd DB state. The policy in beadle-zq6 says 'session-authored bead entries should be scrubbed, older bead entries are left as historical' but the hook defeats that policy by rewriting historical records on every flush.

## Required changes

1. Scrub owner field from bd DB for all historical bead records, or switch bd to use a non-PII handle (e.g., 'claude', 'jmf') consistently.
2. Harden the pre-commit hook to reject commits that would introduce owner PII to the JSONL — either by stripping owner fields before flush or by failing loudly with an actionable message.
3. Consider excluding .beads/issues.jsonl from the pre-commit hook's auto-add entirely: keep bd sync flushing the file, but let the developer decide which changes to stage.

## Verification

After fix: a PR that touches one bead should not introduce owner-field diffs on unrelated beads. git show HEAD:.beads/issues.jsonl | grep -c '@' (for email literals in owner) should return 0 for newly landed commits.

## Blast radius

bd DB + .git/hooks/pre-commit. No Go code changes. Affects all sibling Punt Labs projects that use beads with the same pre-commit hook pattern.

title: Identity entity: multi-identity support for beadle
description: Beadle reads identity from ethos sidecar (DES-001). Core fields (email, name, handle) from ~/.punt-labs/ethos/identities/<handle>.yaml. Beadle-specific data (gpg_key_id, contact permissions) stored in ethos extension mechanism (DES-008) at <handle>.ext/beadle.yaml — beadle owns this file, ethos assembles merged view but never interprets it. Access via 'ethos ext set/get <handle> beadle <key> <value>'. Multi-identity = switching ethos active identity. Beadle maps email to IMAP config internally. No identity management inside beadle. No ethos schema changes.

title: feat(ethos): propose inputs.trigger field for mission contracts
description:
## Problem

When beadle creates a mission triggered by an email, there is no standard field in the ethos mission contract to record the provenance (which email, from whom, what subject).

## Proposal

Add inputs.trigger to the mission contract schema. Ethos stores it as metadata without validating contents. Structure:

    inputs:
      trigger:
        type: email | cron | manual | pipeline
        message_id: "1205"
        from: jim@punt-labs.com
        subject: "Schedule a team meeting"

Audit value: trace a mission back to its trigger without reading the daemon's logs.

Confirmed with ethos agent (biff conversation 2026-04-12): ethos is receptive to this as a metadata field.

## Depends on

- Ethos DES-031 (mission contracts)

## See also

- docs/orchestrator-design.md

title: fix(install): add uninstall before install to enable upgrades
description:
install.sh:144 calls 'claude plugin install beadle@punt-labs --scope user' but never calls uninstall first. Re-running install.sh therefore does not upgrade an existing install — 'claude plugin install' is a no-op when the plugin is already in the cache.

Verified 2026-04-07 on jfreeman's machine: cache stuck at ~/.claude/plugins/cache/punt-labs/beadle/0.8.0/ with gitCommitSha 0226725 (v0.8.0), even though marketplace clone has beadle.ref=v0.9.0 and the binary path was upgraded to v0.9.0 by re-running install.sh. The hook deploys commands from the locked v0.8.0 cache, so /beadle:inbox runs the rejected CronCreate logic instead of v0.9.0's set_poll_interval flow.

Sibling pattern: biff/install.sh:138, quarry/install.sh:175, vox/install.sh:169 all do 'claude plugin uninstall' immediately before 'claude plugin install'. beadle diverges.

Fix: add 'claude plugin uninstall "beadle@$MARKETPLACE_NAME" < /dev/null 2>/dev/null || true' immediately before the install call at install.sh:145. Match biff/quarry/vox phrasing.

Companion work: punt-kit standards do not document this pattern (distribution.md only says 'Idempotent — safe to re-run. Upgrades if already installed' without specifying how). Separate PR to punt-kit will add the explicit bullet so other projects don't reinvent the same gap.

title: Claude Agento GPG key violates non-expiring-keys invariant
description:
## Problem

The agent's GPG key (used for signing outbound email and verifying identity) has no expiration date. This violates the non-expiring-keys-rejected invariant defined in CLAUDE.md: 'All command-signing keys must have an expiration date. This is a security invariant.'

## Required Fix

Generate a new GPG key for claude@punt-labs.com with a 1-year expiry. Update the ethos identity record with the new key ID. Document key rotation procedure.

## Context

This is a prerequisite for beadle-atz (outbound PGP signing) and the Docker deployment concept, where each agent instance would generate a fresh key with expiry on first run. The fix here covers the dev/prod claude@punt-labs.com key specifically.

## Acceptance Criteria

- claude@punt-labs.com GPG key has an expiration date
- Ethos identity record updated
- Key rotation procedure documented

title: feat(mcp): channels support — push inbox notifications as prompts
description:
## Problem

The two-layer polling design (DES-015) has a sync gap: the MCP server detects new mail but relies on a durable CronCreate job in Claude Code for processing. These can drift (7-day expiry, manual intervention, session crashes). Channels eliminates this gap.

## Solution

Declare experimental['claude/channel'] in beadle-email's MCP capabilities. When the poller detects new unread mail, fire notifications/claude/channel with content that triggers inbox processing. Claude Code picks it up as a real prompt between turns. One layer, perfect sync, no CronCreate needed.

## Two modes of operation

Channels is a research preview (shipped 2026-03-20, claude.ai Pro+ only, GrowthBook-gated, gradual rollout). Not all users have access. beadle-email must support both:

Mode A (channels available): Poller fires notifications/claude/channel. No CronCreate needed. Detection and processing in one layer.

Mode B (channels unavailable): Poller fires tools/list_changed only. User sets up /loop 5m /inbox 5m for CronCreate processing layer. Two layers, best-effort sync. This is the current shipping behavior.

The server should detect at startup whether the client supports channels (capability negotiation during MCP initialize handshake) and auto-select the mode. No user configuration needed — if the client declares channel support, use it; otherwise fall back to tools/list_changed.

## Implementation

1. internal/mcp/server.go (or wherever MCP server is configured):
   - Declare experimental['claude/channel'] in server capabilities
   - During initialize, check if client declared channel support

2. internal/mcp/poller.go (or wherever the poller goroutine lives):
   - When new mail detected and channels available: fire notifications/claude/channel with content like "N new messages in inbox. Process with /inbox."
   - When channels unavailable: fire tools/list_changed (existing behavior)

3. Channel notification content design:
   - Must not be a slash command (skipSlashCommands: true on channel messages)
   - Content should prompt the model to run /inbox, e.g.: "3 new unread messages arrived from: Jim Freeman, GitHub Notifications. Check your inbox."
   - Include meta fields: { source: "beadle-email", type: "inbox_alert", unread_count: "3" }

4. Plugin manifest (plugin.json):
   - Declare channels capability so claude --channels plugin:beadle@punt-labs works

## User experience

### With channels (Mode A)
    claude --channels plugin:beadle@punt-labs
    # That's it. Poller detects mail, pushes channel notification, Claude acts.

### Without channels (Mode B, current)
    # User runs /loop 5m /inbox 5m to set up both layers manually.
    # Or /inbox 5m if CronCreate already exists.

## Requirements

- claude.ai Pro+ subscription (OAuth, not API key)
- Claude Code v2.1.80+ (channels support)
- Channels flag on launch: --channels plugin:beadle@punt-labs

## Acceptance criteria

- When channels available: poller fires channel notification, Claude processes inbox without CronCreate
- When channels unavailable: falls back to tools/list_changed, existing behavior preserved
- Mode detection is automatic (capability negotiation), no user config
- Channel notification includes sender summary and unread count

title: epic: headless GPG email agent (Docker)
description:
## Goal

A Docker image that runs beadle as a headless, always-on email agent. No Proton Bridge required. Trust is established via GPG: inbound messages are verified by signature and decrypted, outbound replies are signed and encrypted to the recipient's key. Full bidirectional E2E encryption is the target, not a nice-to-have.

## Delivery

When complete, a user can run:

  docker compose up

...and have a fully autonomous email agent that:
- Polls their inbox on a schedule
- Replies to rw- contacts with GPG-signed, encrypted responses
- Executes instructions from rwx contacts (GPG-verified sender required)
- Decrypts inbound encrypted mail addressed to the agent's key
- Encrypts outbound to recipients whose key ID is in contacts

## Children

- beadle-72e: valid agent GPG key (prerequisite for all signing/decryption)
- beadle-0ut: smtp_host config (removes Bridge dependency)
- beadle-atz: outbound PGP signing
- beadle-ksk: inbound PGP decryption
- beadle-oep: outbound encryption to recipient's key (required, not optional)
- beadle-o1w: Docker image (Dockerfile, docker-compose, init script)

title: Contact matching by email domain/pattern (e.g. *@mail.anthropic.com)
description:
Anthropic (and likely others) use per-message randomized sender
addresses (e.g. no-reply-<random>@mail.anthropic.com). The current
contact system matches by exact email address, so each new Anthropic
message arrives from an unknown address and gets blocked as '---'. We
need domain-based or glob-pattern contact matching so a single contact
entry like '*@mail.anthropic.com' can cover all messages from a given
sender. This would also handle other senders that rotate addresses
within a known domain.

Design constraint (CEO direction 2026-04-09): domain-pattern contacts
are restricted to r-- (read-only) permission ONLY. Patterns cannot be
granted rw- or rwx. Whitelisting rwx on a whole domain is unsafe —
anyone who can send mail from that domain (or spoof it) would inherit
reply/write/execute authority. r-- is the maximum for a pattern match.

Match-order rules:
1. Exact-address contacts always take precedence over pattern
   contacts, whether permissive or restrictive. A specific
   attacker@mail.anthropic.com with --- beats a permissive
   *@mail.anthropic.com with r--.
2. Patterns are evaluated only after exact lookup misses.
3. Longest-prefix (or most-specific) pattern wins among pattern
   matches. '*@ci.vercel.app' beats '*@vercel.app' for a sender at
   ci.vercel.app.
4. If no pattern matches, fall through to the default --- for
   unknown senders.

Validation: add_contact / contacts.json schema must reject any
pattern-style entry with permissions other than r--. CLI and MCP
tool return an explicit error naming the rule.

Requires a DESIGN.md ADR documenting the rwx-only-for-exact rule and
the precedence order, building on DES-012 (two-party identity trust
model).

Concrete use cases:
- *@mail.anthropic.com r-- — Anthropic announce/status/marketing
- *@github.com r-- — GitHub notification relays (including
  notifications@github.com and per-user bot addresses)
- *@amazonses.com r-- — SES-sent transactional mail from SaaS
- *@sendgrid.net r-- — SendGrid-sent transactional mail
notes: Merged in PR #111. Glob-pattern contact matching with r-- constraint (patterns may not grant w or x) and exact>longest-pattern>default precedence via new store.FindByAddress. Uses path.Match from stdlib — supports *, ?, and [ character classes. 18 unit tests + 5 handler tests including negative controls. DES-019 documents the design. Three Copilot review findings addressed in c2d5709 (IsPattern now detects [ bracket classes, tool descriptions honestly say 'r-- or ---' instead of claiming 'r-- only', tie-break test rewritten with genuine equal-length overlapping patterns).

title: feat(email): PGP-sign outbound email
description:
## Problem

Beadle sends email unsigned. Recipients cannot verify that a reply came from the agent and wasn't spoofed. This is the missing half of the bidirectional GPG trust model.

## Scope Change

Original scope was SES-based. Updated: use standard SMTP (no SES dependency). The agent's existing GPG key signs outbound email as a PGP/MIME multipart/signed message.

## Required Changes

- Sign outgoing email body with the agent's GPG key (gpg --armor --detach-sign)
- Produce a valid multipart/signed MIME structure
- Use the identity's GPG key ID (from ethos or config)
- Verify the key has an expiry before signing (non-expiring keys are rejected per invariant)
- Works with any SMTP transport (Bridge or standard)

## Acceptance Criteria

- send_email produces a multipart/signed message
- Recipient can verify with gpg --verify
- Unsigned outbound is blocked if agent key is missing or non-expiring
- Tests: round-trip sign/verify using ephemeral GPG keypair

## Depends On

beadle-72e (agent GPG key must have valid expiry)
close_reason: Merged in PR #132 (squash 9f99b97e). Outbound PGP signing via multipart/signed (RFC 3156). Signing opt-in via gpg_signer config. Resend fallback blocked when signing configured. CRLF canonicalization, SMTP error propagation, doctor gating all addressed in review.

title: permissions: Write/Edit allow rules missing from project settings.json — blocks subagent delegation
description:
beadle/.claude/settings.json permissions.allow lists ~100 entries covering Bash subcommands, MCP tools, and Skill invocations but has ZERO Write or Edit rules. The lead session's Write/Edit calls work because Claude Code's interactive approval flow applies to the foreground session. Background subagents (delegated via Agent tool with run_in_background:true) hit the same approval prompt and silent-deny because there is no interactive context to approve.

Discovered 2026-04-08 when delegating beadle-0he to bwk. bwk's subagent context successfully ran Read/Grep/Glob, completed a full codebase analysis, produced a drop-in-ready patch for internal/mcp/format.go + new format_test.go + CHANGELOG entry, then could not apply any of it because Write/Edit/Bash-with-writes were denied. bwk's own .claude/agents/bwk.md correctly declares 'tools: [Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob]' — the agent definition is not the problem. The project settings.json allow list is.

Workaround in place: path-scoped Write/Edit rules added to .claude/settings.local.json (gitignored). This unblocks the current machine but does not help any other developer who clones beadle and tries to delegate to bwk.

Proper fix: add bare Read/Write/Edit rules to the project-level .claude/settings.json (committed). Similar rules likely missing across other punt-labs projects.
notes: Root cause: .claude/settings.json allow list was missing bare Read/Write/Edit tool-name rules. Sub-agents inherit project-level permissions from settings.json; without bare rules they default to prompt tier which silently denies in background execution. Path-scoped forms (Read(<repo>/**), Read(../**)) are not honored by sub-agents — only the bare tool-name form works. Fix: three bare entries added to settings.json via /permissions slash command. Verified by direct sub-agent probe (Read+Bash+Edit all succeed after fix). Follow-up Bugbot catch: bare Read exposes .env/.envrc read; added Read(.env)/Read(.envrc)/Bash(cat .env:*) denies. Empirically verified path-scoped denies DO override bare allow for sub-agents (asymmetric to allow). Standards updated in punt-kit#209. Third incident of this bug (prior: 2026-04-05, 2026-04-06).

title: Command naming design: resolve namespace conflicts with biff/vox, define beadle-email commands
description:
Three command verbs for beadle-email:

/mail — 'Mail this to me/someone.' Email-specific outbound. Always means email. Examples: '/mail me a summary', '/mail this to kai@example.com'.

/inbox — 'Process your inbox.' Beadle checks its own inbox for owner instructions/context. Examples: '/inbox', '/inbox check for anything from me'.

/send — 'Send via any channel.' Multi-channel outbound, email-only today but designed for Signal etc. Examples: '/send me an email', '/send me a link on Signal'.

Today /mail and /send overlap (both send email). They diverge when new channels arrive: /send routes by channel, /mail always means email.

/read stays with biff today. Future: becomes multi-channel inbox (biff + email + Signal).

Namespace: beadle:mail, beadle:inbox, beadle:send as plugin-scoped. /mail, /inbox, /send as top-level deployed commands. No conflicts with existing biff/vox/quarry/z-spec commands.

MCP server key: 'email' (adds meaning like biff uses 'tty', dungeon uses 'grimoire'). Tool name simplification (send_email → send, etc.) deferred.
close_reason: Command naming resolved: /mail (email outbound), /inbox (process inbox), /send (multi-channel). Documented in DESIGN.md DES-010.

fix(inbox): set_poll_interval must also create CronCreate loop for autonomous processing
description:
## Problem

set_poll_interval persists the interval and starts the MCP poller, but the poller only sends tools/list_changed notifications. Nothing autonomously invokes /inbox — the feature only works when the user is interactive.

## Required Changes

1. commands/inbox.md — when a valid interval is received (5m, 10m, 15m, 30m, 1h, 2h): after calling set_poll_interval, also create a CronCreate job at the same interval to run /inbox. When 'n' is received: also delete the existing cron job.

2. Session-start behavior — at session start, call get_poll_status; if Active=true and interval is set, recreate the CronCreate job (session-scoped jobs don't survive Claude Code restarts).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Setting a poll interval creates both the MCP poller and a CronCreate loop
- Disabling polling (n) removes both
- After Claude Code restart, session-start check recreates the cron job if polling is configured
- /inbox is invoked autonomously at the configured interval regardless of user presence

title: beadle-email doctor should detect unprotected GPG keys
description:
Today doctor unconditionally reports '[!] gpg_passphrase credential not found' whenever the gpg-passphrase secret is absent. This is wrong for keys that have no passphrase set — doctor treats a non-issue as a failure, causing doctor to exit 1 and masking real problems.

Proposed behavior: at doctor run, probe the configured signing key (cfg.GPGSigner) with gpg --batch --pinentry-mode=error --dry-run --sign and classify:
- Key needs passphrase and secret is set → [+] gpg_passphrase
- Key needs passphrase and secret missing → [!] gpg_passphrase credential not found
- Key has no passphrase → [+] gpg_passphrase not required (unprotected key) OR a separate warning '[!] signing key unprotected'

Second option is arguably better because it surfaces the unprotected state rather than hiding it. That aligns with the 'gpg is a real part of the system' principle — unprotected signing keys are a posture concern worth reporting, not silencing.

Related: beadle-72e (Claude key has no expiration AND no passphrase).

Scope:
- cmd/beadle-email/admin_cmd.go (doctor handler): add the probe and three-way classification
- Possibly new helper in internal/pgp/ (e.g. pgp.KeyHasPassphrase(signer string) (bool, error))
- Tests with a sandboxed GNUPGHOME containing a passphraseless test key
- Update docs/setup-guide.md doctor output table

title: Security hardening: timeouts, TLS, path traversal
description:
PR #1 review feedback. Five related hardening issues:

1. `secret.Get()` should reject path separators in name and enforce file permissions before reading
2. IMAP `Dial()` uses `net.Dial` with no timeout — use `net.DialTimeout`
3. IMAP/SMTP `InsecureSkipVerify` should only be allowed for loopback addresses
4. `Send()` uses `http.DefaultClient` with no timeout — create client with 30s timeout
5. SMTP `net.Dial` needs timeout, magic number `2*1e9` should be `2*time.Second`

Files: `internal/secret/secret.go`, `internal/email/imap.go`, `internal/email/smtp.go`, `internal/email/send.go`

title: Code quality: typed responses, UTF-8 truncation, boundary parsing

PR #1 review feedback. Three code quality issues:

1. `tools.go` uses `map[string]any` for tool responses — define typed structs per CLAUDE.md Go standards
2. `truncate()` in `mime.go` uses byte slicing — splits multi-byte UTF-8 runes. Use rune-aware slicing.

title: feat(docker): full agent stack — ethos, biff, quarry, vox in compose
description:
## Problem

The current Docker image (beadle-o1w) runs only beadle-email. When Claude Code runs inside the container (beadle-vyv), it needs the full Punt Labs agent stack for autonomous operation.

## Required Tools

- ethos: identity resolution, session roster, whoami (Go binary)
- biff: team coordination, /who, /read, /write, /plan (Go CLI + Python MCP)
- quarry: semantic search, /find, context recall (Python + ONNX model)
- vox: voice synthesis for audio email attachments (Python + TTS engine)

## Use Case: Voice Email

beadle detects mail → processes inbox → generates summary → vox synthesizes audio → beadle sends email with .mp3 attachment. A headless agent that sends voice memos over email.

## Architecture Options

A) Fat image: everything in one container. Simple, large (~500 MB+).
B) Compose stack: each tool is its own service, connected via mcp-proxy WebSocket. Matches quarry's existing daemon pattern. Each image stays focused.

Option B preferred — quarry already runs as a separate daemon, vox can do the same. mcp-proxy handles the N:1 connection from Claude Code to all services.

## Depends On

- beadle-o1w (Docker image, done)
- beadle-vyv (orchestrator — Claude Code spawned by beadle)

title: feat(pgp): inbound PGP decryption for encrypted messages
description:
## Problem

Beadle handles multipart/signed (signature verification) but not multipart/encrypted. Without Proton Bridge, there is no transparent decryption layer. Users who encrypt messages to the agent's public key get unreadable content.

## Required Changes

- Detect multipart/encrypted MIME structure in incoming messages
- Decrypt using agent's GPG private key (gpg --decrypt in isolated GNUPGHOME)
- Surface decrypted body to read_message and list_messages
- Trust classification: encrypted + verified sig → verified; encrypted only → unverified (can't confirm sender without sig)
- Handle encrypted+signed (the common case: encrypt-then-sign or sign-then-encrypt)
- Agent's public key must be discoverable (WKD or keyserver) so senders can encrypt to it

## Acceptance Criteria

- read_message decrypts messages encrypted to agent's key
- Encrypted + signed messages show correct trust level
- Encrypted-only messages show unverified trust
- Tests: round-trip encrypt/decrypt using ephemeral GPG keypair

## Depends On

beadle-72e (valid agent key required for decryption)
close_reason: Merged in PR #134 (squash c373d8c3). Inbound PGP decryption via gpg --decrypt with system keyring. parseMessage auto-decrypts multipart/encrypted before ParseMIME. 6 tests. CLAUDE.md workflow updates bundled.

feat(docker): Docker image for headless GPG email agent
description:
## Concept

A Docker image that bundles the claude CLI and beadle-email binary, configured to run as a headless always-on email agent. Users bring their own IMAP/SMTP credentials and GPG key. No Proton Bridge required.

## Components

### Dockerfile

- Base: alpine
- Packages: gnupg, ca-certificates
- Binaries: beadle-email (from GitHub releases), claude CLI (npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code)
- Entrypoint: cron daemon running agent loop

### Agent loop

Every N minutes (configurable, default 30m):
  claude --print --model claude-opus-4-6 'process my inbox: check for new messages, reply to rw- contacts, execute instructions from rwx contacts'

The system prompt embeds the beadle permission model. No persistent Claude Code session needed.

### docker-compose.yml

Services:
- agent: the main container, with volumes for GPG keyring and config

### First-run init (docker compose run agent init)

1. Prompt for IMAP host/port/user/password
2. Prompt for SMTP host/port/user/password  
3. Generate agent GPG keypair with 1-year expiry OR import existing private key
4. Store credentials via secret file (mode 600) or environment
5. Print agent public key for the user to distribute to contacts
6. Run beadle-email doctor to verify

### Volumes

- /data/gnupg — GPG keyring (persistent)
- /data/config — beadle config and contacts
- /data/attachments — downloaded attachments

## Acceptance Criteria

- docker compose up starts the agent loop
- Agent processes email autonomously per the permission model
- Outbound replies are GPG-signed (requires beadle-atz)
- Inbound signed mail is verified; inbound encrypted mail is decrypted (requires beadle-ksk)
- README documents setup in under 10 steps

## Depends On

beadle-0ut (smtp_host config), beadle-atz (outbound signing), beadle-72e (valid key), beadle-ksk (inbound decryption)

title: feat(pgp): encrypt outbound email to recipient's GPG key
description:
## Problem

Beadle signs outbound email (beadle-atz) but does not encrypt it. For users who have stored a GPG key ID in their contact record, beadle should encrypt replies to that key so only the intended recipient can read them.

## Required Changes

- When sending to a contact with a gpg_key_id, fetch their public key and encrypt the message body
- Produce a valid multipart/encrypted MIME structure (RFC 3156)
- If the recipient's key is not in the keyring, fall back to signed-only (do not fail silently — warn)
- Encrypt-then-sign (standard PGP convention)
- Key lookup: check isolated GNUPGHOME first, then system keyring, then optionally WKD

## Acceptance Criteria

- Replies to contacts with gpg_key_id are encrypted + signed
- Replies to contacts without gpg_key_id are signed only
- Missing key warning surfaced in tool response
- Tests: round-trip encrypt+sign / decrypt+verify using ephemeral keypairs

## Depends On

beadle-atz (outbound signing must exist first), beadle-ksk (symmetric with inbound decryption)

title: fix(mime): read_message fails on nested multipart bodies (inline images, HTML)
description:
## Problem

read_message returns '(no text body)' for emails with nested multipart structures. When GPG Mail sends multipart/signed containing multipart/mixed (text + images), the MIME parser only looks for text/plain at the top level. The text and images are one level deeper.

Affects: any email with inline images, HTML-only bodies, or GPG Mail signed replies.

## Root cause

ParseMIME in internal/email/mime.go does not recurse into nested multipart parts. download_attachment returns raw MIME subtrees rather than individual decoded parts.

## Acceptance criteria

- read_message extracts text/plain from any nesting depth
- show_mime displays the full recursive MIME tree
- download_attachment extracts individual leaf parts, not raw multipart subtrees

Discovered during PGP signing manual tests 2026-04-11 — had to use Python to extract screenshots from Jim's email.
close_reason: Merged in PR #135 (squash 33086422). ParseMIME now recurses into nested multipart via walkParts helper. 3 tests at 2-3 levels of nesting.

title: Address book: resolve recipients by name instead of raw email address

title: feat(smtp): Fastmail as recommended SMTP transport for PGP signing
description:
## Finding

Tested 2026-04-11: Fastmail SMTP (smtp.fastmail.com:465, implicit TLS) preserves multipart/signed envelopes. GPG Mail on macOS shows 'Signed (claude@punt-labs.com)' with green checkmark. Proton Bridge and Resend/SES both strip multipart/signed and deliver signature.asc as a regular attachment — GPG Mail does not auto-verify.

## Required

To send as claude@punt-labs.com via Fastmail (not claude_puntlabs@pobox.com), add punt-labs.com as a verified sending identity in the Fastmail account. Requires DNS TXT record for domain verification. SPF/DKIM may also need updating.

## Context

Test config saved at ~/.punt-labs/beadle/identities/claude@punt-labs.com/email.json.fastmail-test. Fastmail app password in pass beadle/fastmail-app-password. See CLAUDE.md Fastmail Test Config section.

`TestIMAPPassword_Resolves` and `TestResendAPIKey_Resolves` call real `secret.Get()` chain and assert no error. Fails on any machine without credentials configured. Use `t.Setenv` with test values instead.

title: format: cap total FROM cell width to bound row growth after z34 annotation
description:
After beadle-z34 merged, the FROM column can render up to 39 runes wide in the worst case: 20-rune display name (capped by maxDisplayNameRunes) + ' (via ' (6) + 12-rune domain label (maxRelayLabelRunes) + ')' (1) = 39. Combined with the EMAIL column added in beadle-0he, a long-name relay row can blow past the 80-col soft budget.

Proposed fix: cap the total FROM cell width, not just the display-name portion. Split budget: e.g. 28 runes total for the FROM cell. When the annotation would push past that, truncate the name further to fit. Precedence: the '(via <domain>)' suffix is more important than the name's last few characters, because the disambiguation is the entire point of the annotation.

Suggested implementation:
- New const maxFromCellRunes = 28 (or similar, tunable).
- formatFromCell computes the annotation first, subtracts its width from maxFromCellRunes, uses the remainder as the display-name budget (not maxDisplayNameRunes).
- When the annotation is absent, the existing maxDisplayNameRunes cap still applies.

Test additions:
- A 25-rune name + relay annotation — name must be truncated further than maxDisplayNameRunes to fit the annotation.
- A 10-rune name + relay annotation — no additional truncation needed.

Filed as follow-up from pre-PR review of beadle-z34 by feature-dev:code-reviewer. The reviewer's exact wording: 'Consider capping the total FROM cell (name + annotation) to a fixed budget (e.g., 32 runes) to bound the column width regression relative to 0he.'

Non-blocking for z34 ship — the current behavior is cosmetically wide but functionally correct. The variable SUBJECT column absorbs the excess via formatTable's existing clamp logic.

Related:
- beadle-0he: introduced the EMAIL column (this PR adds to the total row width)
- beadle-z34: introduced the relay annotation (the new width contribution)
notes: Obsolete. The z34 (via <domain>) annotation this bead was capping was deleted in DES-018 (PR #106). The new FROM column already enforces a total width via formatFromCell's name-cap logic (37 − len(email) − 3), so there is no unbounded growth to bound.

title: design: beadle as orchestrator — MCP server spawns Claude Code for inbox processing
description:
## Problem

The current two-layer polling design (DES-015) has a sync gap: the MCP server detects new mail (background goroutine) but relies on a durable CronCreate job in Claude Code for processing. These can drift — CronCreate expires after 7 days, manual intervention breaks sync, session crashes leave one layer orphaned.

In the Docker sandbox model, this gets worse: who starts Claude Code? Today Claude Code starts beadle. In a sandbox, beadle is the long-running daemon.

## Solution

Invert the launch. beadle spawns Claude Code with a prompt file:

    claude --prompt-file .beadle/startup.md

Where startup.md is:

    /loop 5m /inbox 5m

This single command sets both layers atomically:
1. /loop 5m creates the durable CronCreate job that fires /inbox every 5m (processing layer)
2. /loop immediately executes /inbox 5m on the first fire
3. /inbox 5m calls set_poll_interval → MCP server background goroutine starts (detection layer)

Both layers in sync from the first turn. No SessionStart hooks, no CLAUDE.md instructions, no hoping the model acts. The prompt IS a user message — the model always processes it.

## Sandbox startup sequence

    sbx run claude --prompt-file .beadle/startup.md

Or beadle spawns it internally:

    exec.Command("claude", "--prompt-file", promptPath)

The sync problem disappears because there is no gap between "server starts" and "processing starts." One atomic launch.

## Open Questions

- Does claude --prompt-file exist today? What flags control headless/batch execution?
- Can beadle inside a sandbox spawn claude inside the same sandbox?
- What happens when the Claude Code session ends (context limit, crash)? Does beadle detect and re-launch?
- Does this violate "zero agent authority"? The owner's signed config (poll_interval in email.json) is the authorization. The prompt file is a static, auditable artifact.
- How does this interact with N:1 multi-session via mcp-proxy? The prompt-file session is the "processing" session; interactive sessions connect separately.

## Relationship to Current Work

- Not blocking beadle-o1w (Docker image). Ship the current two-layer design first.
- This is a future architecture for when beadle runs as a long-lived sandbox daemon.
- The PreToolUse sync-check hook is the interim enforcement for the current design.

title: Add mid-session identity switching via MCP tool
format: relayDomainLabel picks wrong label for PSL-style TLDs (.co.uk etc.)
description:
relayDomainLabel in internal/mcp/format.go uses a naive 'second-to-last dot-separated label' rule to pick the registrable label from an email domain. This gives the right answer for 2- and 3-label domains (github.com → github, ci.vercel.app → vercel) but the wrong answer for Public Suffix List (PSL) multi-label TLDs:

  x@example.co.uk → labels ['example', 'co', 'uk'] → picks 'co' (wrong, should be 'example')
  x@a.b.example.co.uk → labels ['a', 'b', 'example', 'co', 'uk'] → picks 'co' (wrong)

The relay annotation for such senders would render as '(via co)' instead of '(via example)', which is confusing but not security-relevant.

Documented as a known limitation in the patch code comments and the z34 bwk design notes. Filed here as a standalone bead so it's tracked.

Proposed fix: depend on a PSL library or table. Go has several options:
- golang.org/x/net/publicsuffix — stdlib-adjacent, maintained, small dependency
- A vendored PSL data file

For beadle's use case (displaying relay annotations), even a small hardcoded list of common PSL exceptions (co.uk, com.au, co.jp, etc.) would cover 99% of real traffic without the dependency weight.

Test cases that would verify the fix:
- x@example.co.uk → 'example'
- x@example.com.au → 'example'
- x@a.example.co.jp → 'example'

Non-blocking. The current heuristic is correct for the primary targets (github.com, vercel.app, resend.com, etc.).
notes: Obsolete. relayDomainLabel was deleted in DES-018 (PR #106) along with the rest of the z34 relay-annotation helpers. The PSL-bug this bead tracked no longer exists in the code.

title: fix(daemon): migrate contract from inputs.ticket to inputs.trigger
feat(rwx): enforce x-bit — mission authorization for email-triggered actions
description:
## Problem

The x bit in DES-012's rwx permission model has been declared but never enforced. Contacts with x permission can theoretically trigger autonomous actions, but no code checks x before taking action.

## Solution

When beadle's inbox processing detects an email containing an instruction from a contact with x permission, it creates an ethos mission contract. The x bit is the gate: without it, beadle reads and replies but never creates missions.

Enforcement lives in beadle (not ethos). Beadle checks x on the contact, then calls ethos mission create. Ethos validates the contract but doesn't know or care about email permissions.

## Depends on

- beadle-vyv (orchestrator — daemon spawns Claude Code as mission worker)
- ethos Phase 3.4+ (round enforcement, result artifacts)

## See also

- docs/orchestrator-design.md
- DES-012 (rwx model)
- Ethos DES-031 (mission contracts)

title: list_messages FROM column is misleading for notification services
description:
list_messages shows the display name from the From header without any indication that the actual sender is a notification relay. For GitHub in particular, every notification uses From: 'J Freeman <notifications@github.com>' or 'Copilot <notifications@github.com>' where the display name varies by who triggered the event but the email is always notifications@github.com.

Verified 2026-04-08 during beadle-1aj troubleshooting: 285 unread messages in the claude@punt-labs.com inbox, listed under 7 distinct display names (J Freeman, Copilot, cursor[bot], vercel[bot], Claude Agento, github-actions[bot], claude[bot]). Every single message actually came from notifications@github.com. The display names were misleading: 'J Freeman' appears as a sender because Jim (jim@punt-labs.com) triggered a GitHub event, not because Jim sent beadle an email. 'Claude Agento' similarly shows up for events I triggered via my claude-puntlabs GitHub account.

Impact:
- Readers (human or agent) naturally trust the display name and act on it as the sender identity. In beadle's permission model this is wrong: the permission lookup uses the EMAIL not the display name, so the Jim Freeman contact with rwx does not grant permission on 'J Freeman' messages because those are actually notifications@github.com.
- Contact bootstrap appears to need 7 contacts but actually needs 1 (notifications@github.com with r--).
- Agents reading list_messages output can misattribute messages to the wrong identity.

Proposed fix (pair with beadle-<other-one-filed-alongside-this>):

1. list_messages FROM column shows 'J Freeman via github.com' or 'J Freeman (notifications@github.com)' for messages where the From email domain does not match the claimed display-name identity.
2. For known notification-relay senders (notifications@github.com, vercel, resend, sendgrid, etc.), list_messages could add a 'NOTIFY' badge or similar marker.
3. Simpler: always show the email next to the display name. Addresses both this bead and the discoverability bead.

Related: display-name spoofing is a real security concern. A phishing email with From: 'Jim Freeman <attacker@evil.com>' would show up as 'Jim Freeman' in the FROM column of list_messages today. The agent would correctly NOT grant it Jim's permissions (since email lookup is by address), but a human skimming the list might trust it. Surfacing the email alongside the display name is a defense in depth against display-name spoofing.

Filed during beadle-1aj troubleshooting 2026-04-08 on the same 285-message backlog that exposed it.

title: .claude/scheduled_tasks.json should be gitignored
description: CronCreate with durable:true writes to .claude/scheduled_tasks.json. The file is machine-local cron persistence state, not repo config. .gitignore includes .claude/scheduled_tasks.lock but not the .json. After /inbox 10m ships the durable cron in PR #128, any session that re-runs /inbox 10m leaves an untracked file in the worktree that could be accidentally committed. Add '.claude/scheduled_tasks.json' to .gitignore next to .claude/scheduled_tasks.lock. One-line change.

title: feat(email): support implicit TLS for IMAP (993) and SMTP (465), fix STARTTLS ServerName
description:
## Problem

beadle-email only supports STARTTLS for both IMAP and SMTP. Standard providers (Fastmail, Gmail, Migadu) use implicit TLS on port 993 (IMAP) and 465 (SMTP). Additionally, the SMTP STARTTLS path doesn't set ServerName in the TLS config, causing handshake failures with any non-loopback server.

Three fixes:

1. IMAP implicit TLS (port 993): use imapclient.DialTLS when the port is 993, NewStartTLS otherwise. Add an imap_tls bool config field (default: auto-detect from port).
2. SMTP implicit TLS (port 465): use tls.Dial when the port is 465, STARTTLS otherwise. Add an smtp_tls bool config field (default: auto-detect from port).
3. SMTP ServerName: set ServerName in the TLS config for non-loopback hosts. Already fixed in working tree, needs to be committed.

## Acceptance criteria

- Fastmail IMAP (imap.fastmail.com:993) connects and lists messages
- Fastmail SMTP (smtp.fastmail.com:587) sends signed email with multipart/signed preserved
- SMTP on port 465 (implicit TLS) connects
- Existing Proton Bridge (localhost STARTTLS) behavior unchanged
- Tests pass with -race

## Context

This unblocks Fastmail as a complete agent email provider for the Docker deployment (beadle-o1w). Currently the README suggests imap_port 143 for external providers, which is STARTTLS — most providers prefer or require 993.
close_reason: Merged in PR #133 (squash fddb96c4). Implicit TLS for IMAP 993 and SMTP 465. ServerName fix for STARTTLS. Port-based auto-detection.

title: Scrub owner PII from pre-existing tests and docs
description:
Per CEO directive 2026-04-09: owner PII (owner's name, full name, work email, pobox email, unix username, home-directory path) should not appear in tracked project files. Session-scoped files (DES-018 in DESIGN.md, internal/mcp/format_test.go, CHANGELOG.md [0.10.2]) were cleaned up in PR #109. Pre-existing instances remain in the following files and need a mechanical scrub:

- CLAUDE.md identity block
- DESIGN.md DES-012, DES-013, DES-015 (pre-DES-018 design sections)
- commands/inbox.md owner example
- docs/build-plan-we7.md identity-switch plan fixtures
- prfaq.tex
- internal/email/address_test.go
- internal/identity/resolve_test.go (incl. fixture YAML file name)
- internal/mcp/handler_test.go
- internal/mcp/table_test.go
- internal/mcp/tools_test.go
- Any other test file turned up by a repo-wide grep

Replacement scheme: width-preserving where surrounding mockups require byte-accurate widths, flexible otherwise. Use non-PII placeholder identities consistently (see PR #109 for the precedent pair).

Scope notes:

1. .beads/issues.jsonl is tracked in git but is bead state, not user-facing code/docs. Session-authored bead entries containing literal PII strings should be scrubbed too; older bead entries authored by other sessions are left alone as historical.
2. .punt-labs/ethos/ is a submodule. Ethos identity files legitimately contain the owner's real identity and are out of scope.
3. Git commit metadata is separate from file content and unaffected.
4. Agent identity (Claude Agento, claude@punt-labs.com) is not owner PII and is preserved.

This is a mechanical refactor across ~18 files, delegated to bwk. Tests should still pass after the fixture rename.

Verification: a repo-wide git grep for the PII patterns should return zero hits across tracked files excluding .beads and .punt-labs/ethos.
notes: Merged in PR #110. Owner PII removed from 18 pre-existing tests and docs (CLAUDE.md, DESIGN.md DES-012/013/015, commands/inbox.md, docs/build-plan-we7.md, docs/setup-guide.md, prfaq.tex, internal/contacts/*, internal/email/address_test.go, internal/identity/migrate_test.go, internal/identity/resolve_test.go, internal/mcp/handler_test.go, internal/mcp/table_test.go, internal/mcp/tools_test.go, internal/mcp/identity_tools.go, internal/session/roster_test.go). Grep verification returns zero hits across tracked source/docs/tests excluding .beads and .punt-labs/ethos. Historical bead descriptions containing owner PII are explicitly out of scope per CEO direction — append-only historical record.
